summary: DHH argues that senior leaders have a duty to exercise their judgment and authority rather than retreating into false egalitarianism where all opinions are treated as equally valid. He observes that some senior people become afraid to make decisive calls, hiding behind relativism instead of leveraging their hard-earned competence. This failure to lead hurts junior employees who learn slower without exposure to decisive, experienced decision-making. He clarifies this isn't about blind obedience—questioning authority should always be welcome—but legitimate power rooted in competence must actually be used to be valuable.
key_points: A senior title represents a recognition of competence and experience, which creates a duty to exercise judgment for the benefit of others
Some senior leaders retreat into false relativism where all opinions are treated as equally valid, which helps no one
Junior employees learn less and slower when not exposed to decisive action by experienced people
Exercising legitimate authority is compatible with a culture that allows questioning leadership
The goal is a middle ground between authoritarian command and leaderless egalitarianism
money_quotes: Just because you have a high-ranking title, doesn't mean you're automatically right about everything. But it does mean you should be mostly right, most of the time, or that title isn't vested in legitimate power.

Retreat into some relativistic cave where all opinions, ideas, and insights are presumed equally good, equally deserving of first-principles reasoning, and resulting in an arm's length "who am I to judge" distancing. This helps no one.
Legitimate power drawn from competence and experience is wasted if not exercised.
Junior folks learn less and slower if they are not exposed to decisive action by people who know stuff and have seen stuff.
The organization swerves in its mission if not built on a foundation of decisions and directions made and set by the people most qualified to do so.
We don't have to pick between these extremes.
key_insight: Senior leaders who refuse to exercise their hard-earned judgment out of false egalitarianism fail everyone—their junior colleagues learn less, and the organization drifts without decisive direction.
